title: Folklore the Affliction: Fall of the Spire - Final Adventure Re-Cap and Final Thoughts
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8T1Pe58GJpw

Daniel (@dungeondive) closes out Story One and the *Fall of the Spire* review. Chapter Three runs the heroes through the apothecary and a smaller second city map; the final confrontation with the Anatomist offers multiple ways to locate his dwelling — a classic diamond-pathing structure Daniel praises. He found a *Ring of Mercury* (+1 initiative, +1 stride) and wishes the card said ±1 instead — sometimes you want to *drop* a slot to claim a better bonus.

The headline verdict, and it's a striking one: Folklore the Affliction feels, to Daniel, like *a board-game version of a PlayStation-1-era Japanese RPG*. Overworld map, small pre-rendered adventure tiles, skirmish-versus-map combat split, item economy, character class abilities, side-quest rumors as fetch-and-find micro-adventures. It is the only board game he can think of that scratches that particular nostalgia. That alone earns his patience with the bookkeeping.

The honest criticisms: combat positioning is shallow (maps are mostly pretty backdrops; the line of dice does the work), and the bookkeeping makes slow-questing the game across many short sessions a recipe for forgotten bonuses. His advice: play a whole story or chapter in a single evening. The video closes with a re-boxing demo featuring cardboard box-extenders he borrowed from *Escape From Dulce*.

----

Daniel forgives Folklore's bookkeeping because it scratches a *specific nostalgia* — PS1-era JRPG. Have you ever forgiven serious mechanical friction in a board game purely because *some other feeling* it produces is rare enough to be worth it?
